title: Post With Code V2
text: MDPs are a classical formalisation of sequential decision making, where actions influence not just immediate rewards, but also subsequent situations, or states, and through those future rewards.

title: Post With Code V2
text:
Discount Factor \(\gamma\)
It introduced to model an exponential decay in value of rewards over time. So the value function is \[
V(s) = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\textcolor{red}{\gamma^{t}}R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})
\]
According to geometric series, is the above function is guaranteed to be finite-values as long as the \(|\gamma| < 1\) \[
V(s) \leq \sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^tR_{max} = \frac {R_{max}} { 1- \gamma}
\] where, \(R_{max}\) is the maximum possible reward attainable at any given timestep in the MDP.

[!Info] The \(\gamma\) value is selected strictly from the range \(0 < \gamma < 1\) since values values in the range \(−1 < \gamma ≤ 0\) are simply not meaningful in most real-world situations

title: Post With Code V2
text:
Bellman Equation
The optimal value of a state \(s, V^{*}(s)\) is the expected value of the utility an optimally-behaving (follow policy) agent that starts in \(s\) will receive, over the rest of the agent’s lifetime. The optimal value (Q-value) of a Q-state \(Q^*(s, a)\) is the expected value of the utility an agent receives after starting in \(s\), taking \(a\) and acting optimally henceforth ^q-value






Note



Q-state, also know as action state, is the combine of agent in state \(s\), and take action \(a\)


The Bellman Equation for value function is define as follows: \[
V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s, a, s')\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V*(s')\right]
\] For Q-value function is define as follows: \[
Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} T(s, a, s')\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V*(s')\right]
\] \(R(s, a, s')\) is immediate rewards ^immediate-reward

[!Info] As showing above: \[
V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s, a)
\]

The Bellman equation is an example of a dynamic programming equation. The Q-value is a weighted sum of utilities, with each utility weighted by the probability of\(T(s, a, s')\). This define the expected utility of action optimally from Q-state \((s,a)\)
The \(V^*(s)\) is the maximum expected utility over all possible actions from \(s\).

[!question] BE usage is as a condition for optimality. In other words, if we can somehow determine a value U(s) for every state s ∈ S such that the Bellman equation holds true for each of these states, we can conclude that these values are the optimal values for their respective states. Indeed, satisfying this condition implies ∀s ∈ S, \(V(s) = V^*(s)\)


Value Iteration
To solve the optimal values, we need time-limited values. The time-limited value for a state \(s\) with a time-limit of \(k\) timesteps is denoted \(V_k(s)\), and represents the maximum expected value attainable from \(s\) given that MDP under consideration terminated in \(k\) timesteps.
Value iteration is a dynamic programming algorithm that uses an iteratively longer time limit to compute time-limited values until convergence \[\forall s,\ V_{k+1}(s) = V_k(s)\] It operates as follows: 1. \(\forall s \in S\), initialize \(V_0(s) = 0\). 2. Repeat the following update rule utile convergence: \[
\forall s \in S,\ V_{k + 1}(s) \leftarrow \max_a\sum_{s'}T(s, a, s')\left[R(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s') \right]
\]
The Bellman Equation gives a condition for optimality, while update rule gives a method to iteratively update values util convergence. The above function for short is \[
V_{k + 1} \leftarrow BV_k
\] where \(B\) is called the Bellman operator. which is contraction by \(\gamma\) .

Policy Extraction
The intuition behind policy extraction is very simple: if you’re in a state \(s\), you should take the action \(a\) which yields the maximum expected value.
\[
\forall s \in S, \pi^*(s) = \underset{a}{\mathrm{argmax}}Q^*(s, a)
\]

[!Info] For performance reasons that it’s better for policy extraction to have the optimal Q-values of states, in which case a single argmax operation is all that is required to determine the optimal action from a state.




Q-Value Iteration
Q-value iteration is a dynamic programming algorithm that computes time-limited Q-values. \[
Q_{k+1}(s, a) \leftarrow \sum_{s'}T(s, a, s')\left[R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_k(s', a') \right]
\]


Policy Iteration
Value iteration can be quite slow. At each iteration, we must update the values of all \(|S|\) states, each requires iteration over all \(|A|\) actions. The computation of each of these Q-Values, requires iteration over each of \(|S|\) states again, leading to runtime of \(O(|S|^2|A|)\)
To solve this flaw, we can use policy iteration. It operates as follows: 1. Define an initial policy. 2. Repeat the following until convergence: 1. Evaluate the current policy with policy evaluation. For a policy \(\pi\) , policy evaluation means computing \(V^{\pi}(s)\) for all states \(s\), where \(V^{\pi}\) is expected value of starting in state \(s\) when following \(\pi\): \[
V^{\pi} = \sum_{s'}T(s, \pi(s), s')\left[ R(s, \pi(s), s' )  + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\right]
\] 2. Define the policy at iteration \(i\) as \(\pi_i\). We can updated value function
\[
    V^{\pi_i}_{k+1} \leftarrow \sum_{s'}T(s, \pi(s), s')\left[ R(s, \pi(s), s' )  + \gamma V^{\pi_i}_k (s')\right]
\]
3. Once evaluate the current policy, use **policy improvement** to generate a better policy: 
\[
\pi_{i+1}(s) = \underset{a}{\mathrm{argmax}} \sum_{s'}T(s, a, s')\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi_i}(s') \right]
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
In most every application of machine learning we have to deal with uncertainty, there are two type of uncertainty:
Both uncertainty can be handled using the framework of probability theory. When couple with decision theory, allow us to make optimal predictions given all the information available to us.
:::{.callout-important} Sum Rule & Product Rule
Two most important formulae are:
\[
\begin{gather}
p(X)  = \begin{cases}
\sum_{Y}p(X ,Y) \\ \\
\int p(X,Y) \, dy
\end{cases} &\text{(sum rule)}\\
p(X, Y) = p(Y|X)p(X) &\text{(product rule)}
\end{gather}
\]
The definition of probability in terms of the frequency of repeatable events is the basis of frequentist view of statistics. The use of probability as a quantification of uncertainty if the Bayesian perspective and is more general in that it includes frequentist probability as a special case. The more example we observed, the lower uncertainty we will get

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
2.1 The Rules of Probability
\(X\) represent the presence of absence of cancer, \(Y\) represent the outcome of the test. \(X\) and \(Y\) are called random variables or stochastic variables. \(X\) can take \(x_{i}\) where \(i = 1, \dots, L\) and \(Y\) can take \(j\), where \(j=1, \dots, M\).

\(n_{ij}\): the number of trial that \(X=x_{i}\) and \(Y=y_{j}\)
\(c_{i}\): the number of trial that \(X=x_{i}\), irrespective of the value of \(Y\)
\(r_{j}\): the number of trial that \(Y=y_{j}\), irrespective of the value of \(X\)

So, the sum rule(sometimes called marginal probability) is:
\[
p(X=x_{i}) = \sum_{j=1}^{M}p(X=x_{i}, Y=y_{j})
\]
The conditional probability is \[p(Y=y_{j} | X=x_{i})\] The joint probability is:
\[
p(Y=y_{j}, X=x+i)
\]
And the product rule is:
\[
p(X=x_{i}, Y=y_{j}) = p(Y=y_{j} | X= x_{i})  p(X=x_{i})
\]

2.1.3 Bayes’ theorem
Bayes’ theorem is the relationship between conditional probabilities:
\[p(Y|X) = \frac{p(X|Y)p(Y)}{p(X)}\]
The denominator is the Bayes’ theorem as the normalisation constant.


2.1.4 Medical Screening Example
\(C\) denotes the presence(1) or absence(0) of cancer. We have prior probability:
\[
\begin{gather}
p(C = 1) = 1 / 100 \\
p(C= 0) = 99 / 100
\end{gather}
\]
\(T\) represent the outcome of a screening test. \(T=1\) represent test positive, while \(T=0\) represent test negative. And we known that
\[
\begin{align}
p(T =1 |C=1) = 90 / 100 \\
p(T =0 |C=1) = 10 / 100 \\
p(T =1 |C=0) = 3 / 100 \\
p(T =0 |C=0) = 97 / 100 \\
\end{align}
\]
So, we can use sum rule and product rule to calculate the probability that someone test randomly will get \(T=1\):
\[
p(T=1) = \sum_{c}p(T=1|C=c)p(C=c) = 0.0387
\]
If \(T=1\), then what is \(p(C=1|T=1)\)
\[
p(C=1|T=1) = \frac{p(T=1|C=1)p(C=1)}{p(T=1)} \approx 0.23
\]


2.1.5 Prior and Posterior probabilities


Exercise 2.1

Prior probability \(p(C)\): the probability that available before we observe the result of the test.
Posterior probability \(p(C|T)\): the probability obtained after we observed something.



2.1.6 Independent variables
If the joint distribution of two variables factorised into the product of the marginals:
\[
p(X, Y) = p(X)p(Y)
\]
then, \(X\) and \(Y\) are called independent. And two independent variables has following property from the product rule
\[
\begin{gather}
p(X, Y) = \\  p(Y|X)p(X) =p(X)p(Y) \\
\implies p(Y|X) = p(Y)
\end{gather}
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
2.2 Probability Densities
Probability density \(p(x)\) over a continuous variable \(x\) to be such that the probability of \(x\) falling in the interval \((x, x + \delta x)\) is given by \(p(x)\delta x\) for \(\delta x \to 0\). The probability that \(x\) lie in an interval \((a, b)\) is then given by:
\[
p(x \in (a, b)) = \int ^{b}_{a}p(x) \, dx
\]
The \(p(x)\) must satisfy the two conditions:
\[
\begin{gather}
p(x) \geq  0 \\
\int ^{\infty}_{\infty}p(x) \, dx  = 1
\end{gather}
\]
The probability that \(x\) lies in the interval \((-\infty, z)\) is given by cumulative distribution function:
\[
P(z) = \int _{-\infty}^{z}p(x) \, dx
\]
which satisfies \(P'(x) = p(x)\). If we have several continuous variables \(x_{1}, \dots, x_{D}\), denoted collectively b the vector \(\mathrm{x}\), then we can define a joint probability density \(p(\mathrm{x}) = p(x_{1}, \dots, x_{D})\). This multi-variate probability density must satisfy:
\[
\begin{gather}
p(\mathrm{x}) \geq 0 \\
\int p(\mathrm{x}) \, d\mathrm{x} = 1
\end{gather}
\]
More generally, we can also consider joint probability distribution over a combination of discrete and continuous variables.

2.2.1 Example Distributions


Exercise 2.4
Distributions that cannot be normalised are called improper. We can have a uniform distribution that is constant over a finite region \((c, d)\).
\[
p(x) = 1 / (d - c), \quad x \in (c, d)
\]
Another simple form of density is the exponential distribution:
\[
p(x | \lambda) = \lambda \exp(-\lambda x), \quad x \geq 0
\]


Exercise 2.5
A variant of the exponential distribution, known as the Laplace distribution, all the peak moved to a location \(\mu\) and is given by:
\[
p(x | \mu, \gamma) = \frac{1}{2\gamma}\exp\left( - \frac{{|x - \mu|}}{\gamma}  \right)
\]
Another distribution called Dirac delta function:
\[
p(x | \mu) = \delta(x - \mu)
\]
This is defined to be zero everywhere except \(x = \mu\). If we have a finite set of observations of \(x\) given by \(\mathcal{D} = \{ x_{1}, \dots, x_{N}\}\) then we can use the delta function to construct the empirical distribution given by:
\[
p(x | \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}\delta(x - x_{n})
\]


Exercise 2.6
which consists of Dirac delta function centred on each of the data points.


2.2.2 Expectations and covariances
The weighted average of some function \(f(x)\) under a probability distribution is called expectation of \(f(x)\), denoted by \(\mathbb{E}[f]\)
\[
\mathbb{E}[f] = \begin{cases}
\sum_{x}p(x)f(x), & \text{discrete distribution} \\ \\
\int p(x)f(x) \, dx  & \text{continuous distribution}
\end{cases}
\]


Exercise 2.7
In either case, if we are given a finite number of \(N\) of points drawn from the probability distribution or probability density, then the expectation can be approximated as:
\[
\mathbb{E}[f] \simeq \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}f(x_{n})
\]
Sometimes we will be considering expectations of functions of several variables using:
\[
\mathbb{E}_{x}[f(x, y)]
\]
denote the average of the function of \(f(x, y)\) with respect to the distribution of \(x\). The \(\mathbb{E}_{x}[x,y]\) will be a function of \(y\).


Exercise 2.11
The conditional expectation with respect to a conditional distribution is:
\[
\mathbb{E}_{x}[f|y] = \begin{cases}
\sum_{x}p(x|y)f(x) \\  \\
\int p(x|y) f(x) \, dx
\end{cases}
\]
which is also a function of \(y\).


Exercise 2.8
The variance of function \(f(x)\) is defined as:
\[
\begin{split}
\text{var}[f] &= \mathbb{E}\Big[\big(f(x)-\mathbb{E}[f(x)]\big)^{2}\Big] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[f(x)^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[f(x)]^{2} \\
\end{split}
\]
For two random variable \(x\) and \(y\). the covariance measures the extent to which two variables vary together:
\[
\begin{split}
\text{cov}[x, y] &= \mathbb{E}_{x,y}[ \{ x - \mathbb{E}[x] \} \{ y - \mathbb{E} [y]\}  ] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{x, y}[xy]-\mathbb{E}[x]\mathbb{E}[y] \\
\end{split}
\]


Exercise 2.9
If \(x\) and \(y\) are independent, then their covariance is 0.
For two vector \(x\) and \(y\), their covariance is a matrix:
\[
\begin{split}
\text{cov}[\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}] &= \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}}[\{ \mathbf{x} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}] \} \{ \mathbf{y}^{\intercal}-\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}^{\intercal}] \}  ] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}} [\mathbf{x}\mathbf{y}^{\intercal}] - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}]\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}^{\intercal}]\\
\end{split}
\]


Exercise 2.10
If we consider the covariance of the components of the \(x\) then:
\[
\text{cov}[\mathbf{x}] \equiv \text{cov}[\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}]
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.1



image.png


The prior probability is:
\[
\begin{gather}
p(C=1) = 0.001 \\
p(C= 0) = 0.999
\end{gather}
\]
\[
\begin{align}
p(T =1 |C=1) = 90 / 100 \\
p(T =0 |C=1) = 10 / 100 \\
p(T =1 |C=0) = 3 / 100 \\
p(T =0 |C=0) = 97 / 100 \\
\end{align}
\]
We can calculate
\[
p(T=1) = P(T=1|C=1)p(C=1) + p(T=1|C=0)p(C=0) = 0.03087
\]
So, the Posterior \(p(C|T)\) is:
\[
p(C=1 | T=1) = \frac{p(T=1|C=1)p(C=1)}{p(T=1)} \approx 0.029
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.2



image.png



Yellow Die vs. Red Die:

Probability that Y wins: \(2 / 3\)

R shows 2: \(4 / 6\)
R shows 6: \(0\)


Blue Die vs. Yellow Die

Probability that B wins: \(2 / 3\)

Green Die vs. Blue Die

Probability that G wins: \(2 / 3\)

B shows \(0\): \(2 / 6\)
B shows 4, G need show 5 to win: \(4 / 6 \, \times 3 / 6 =2 /6\)


Red Die vs. Green Die _ Probability that R wins: \(2 / 3\) _ G shows 1: \(3 / 6\) * G shows 5, R need shows 6 to win: \(3 / 6 \, \times 2 / 6 = 1 /6\) Each die in this non-transitive set has a 2/3 probability of rolling a higher number than the previous die in the cycle. This demonstrates the non-transitive property of the Efron dice.

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.3



image.png


Use the law of total probability, we can get that
\[
p_{\mathbf{y}}(\mathbf{y}) = \int^{\infty}_{\infty}p(\mathbf{y|u})p_{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{u})  \, d\mathbf{u}
\]
Since \(\mathbf{y }= \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}\), we have \(\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{u}\), and because \(\mathbf{v}\) and \(\mathbf{u}\) are two independent random variable. So the conditional probability \(p(\mathbf{y | u})\) is the PDF \(p_{\mathbf{v}}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{u})\)
\[
p(\mathbf{y | u}) = p_{\mathbf{v}}(\mathbf{v})= p_{\mathbf{v}}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{u})
\]
Substitute this in, we get:
\[
p(y) = \int^{\infty}_{\infty}p_{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{u}) p_{\mathbf{v}}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{u}) \, d\mathbf{u}
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.4



image.png


Uniform Distribution:
\[
p(x) = 1 / (d - c), \quad x \in (c, d)
\]
Normalisation:
\[
\int^{d}_{c} \frac{1}{d-c} \, dx  = \frac{1}{d-c}\int ^{d}_{c}1 \, dx  = \frac{1}{d-c}(d-c) = 1
\]
Mean: \(\frac{d-c}{2}\) Variance: \((d-c)^{2} / 12\)

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.5
 Exponential distribution:
\[
p(x | \lambda) = \lambda \exp(-\lambda x), \quad x \geq 0
\]
Assume \(u = \lambda x\), where \(u \geq 0\) then \(dx = \frac{{du}}{\lambda}\)
\[
\int^{\infty}_{0} \lambda \exp(-\lambda x)  \, dx  =\int^{\infty}_{0}  \exp(u) du=[-e^{-u}]^{\infty}_{0} = (0-(-1)) = 1
\]
Laplace distribution:
\[
p(x | \mu, \gamma) = \frac{1}{2\gamma}\exp\left( - \frac{{|x - \mu|}}{\gamma}  \right)
\]
$$
\[\begin{split}
\int^{\infty}_{\infty}\frac{1}{2\gamma}\exp\left( - \frac{{|x - \mu|}}{\gamma}  \right) \, dx  = \int^{\infty}_{u}\frac{1}{2\gamma}\exp\left( - \frac{{x - \mu}}{\gamma}  \right) \, dx \\
+ \int^{u}_{-\infty}\frac{1}{2\gamma}\exp\left( - \frac{{\mu -x }}{\gamma}  \right) \, dx
\end{split}\]
$$
Integrate each part separately: For \(x \geq u\), assume \(v = \frac{x-u}{\lambda}\), that \(v \geq 0\) and \(dv = \frac{1}{\lambda}dx\)
\[
\begin{gather}
\int^{\infty}_{u}\frac{1}{2\gamma}\exp\left( - \frac{{x - \mu}}{\gamma}  \right) \, dx \\
= \int^{\infty}_{0} \frac{1}{2}\exp(-v)  \, dv \\
= \frac{1}{2}\int^{\infty}_{0}\exp(-v)  \, dv  \\
= \frac{1}{2}[-e^{-v}]^{\infty}_{0}  \\
= \frac{1}{2}(0 - (-1)) = \frac{1}{2}
\end{gather}
\]
For \(x < u\) assume \(v = \frac{x-u}{\lambda}\), that \(v \leq 0\) and \(dv = \frac{1}{\lambda}dx\)
\[
\begin{gather}
\int^{u}_{-\infty}\frac{1}{2\gamma}\exp\left( - \frac{{\mu - x}}{\gamma}  \right) \, dx \\
= \int^{0}_{-\infty} \frac{1}{2}\exp(v)  \, dv \\
= \frac{1}{2}\int^{0}_{-\infty}\exp(v)  \, dv  \\
= \frac{1}{2}[e^{v}]^{0}_{-\infty}  \\
= \frac{1}{2}(1-0) = \frac{1}{2}
\end{gather}
\]
So the total sum is 1

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.6
 Dirac delta function:
\[
p(x | \mu) = \delta(x - \mu)
\]
Empirical density function:
\[
p(x | \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}\delta(x - x_{n})
\]
The Dirac Delta function has the following properties:

Sifting Property: \[
\int^{\infty}_{-\infty}f(x)\delta(x-\mu)  \, dx  = f(\mu)
\]
Normalisation \[
\int^{\infty}_{-\infty} \delta(x)  \, dx  = 1
\]

Check the normalisation of the empirical density function:
\[
\begin{split}
\int p(x|\mathcal{D}) \, dx  &= \int \left(\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}\delta(x - x_{n})\right) \, dx  \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}\int \delta(x-x_{n}) \, dx  \\
&=  \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}1 \\
&= 1 \\
\end{split}
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.7



image.png


Empirical Density:
\[
p(x) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}\delta(x - x_{n})
\]
\[
\begin{split}
\mathbb{E}[f(x)] &= \int p(x)f(x) \, dx   \\
&= \int f(x) \left( \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\delta(x-x_{n}) \right) \, dx  \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}\int f(x)\delta(x-x_{n}) \, dx &(\text{Sifting Property} )\\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}f(x_{n}) \\
\end{split}
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.8



image.png


\[
\begin{split}
\text{var}[f(x)] &=  \mathbb{E}\Big[\big(f(x)-\mathbb{E}[f(x)]\big)^{2}\Big] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[f(x)^{2} - 2 f(x)\mathbb{E}[f(x)] + \mathbb{E}[f(x)]^{2}]  \\
&= \mathbb{E}[f(x)^{2}] - 2\mathbb{E}\big[f(x)\mathbb{E}[f(x)] \big]  + \mathbb{E}\bigl[ \mathbb{E}[f(x)]^{2} \bigr] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[f(x)^{2}] - 2\mathbb{E}[f(x)]\mathbb{E}[f(x)]   +  \mathbb{E}[f(x)]^{2} \\
&= \mathbb{E}[f(x)^{2}] - 2\mathbb{E}[f(x)]^{2}   +  \mathbb{E}[f(x)]^{2} \\
&= \mathbb{E}[f(x)^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[f(x)]^{2}
\end{split}
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.9
 If \(x\) and \(y\) are independent, then \(\mathbb{E}_{x, y}[xy] = \mathbb{E}[x]\mathbb{E}[y]\)
\[
\begin{split}
\text{cov}[x, y] &= \mathbb{E}_{x,y}[ \{ x - \mathbb{E}[x] \} \{ y - \mathbb{E} [y]\}  ] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{x, y}[xy]-\mathbb{E}[x]\mathbb{E}[y] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[x]\mathbb{E}[y] -  \mathbb{E}[x]\mathbb{E}[y] \\
&= 0 \\
\end{split}
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.10
 Because of the linearity of expectation, this property holds regardless of whether \(x\) and \(z\) are independent or not.
\[
\begin{split}
\text{var}[x + z] &=  \mathbb{E}[(x+z)^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[x+z]^{2} \\
&=  \mathbb{E}[x^{2} + 2xz + z^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[x+z]^{2}  \\
&= \mathbb{E}[x^{2}] + 2\mathbb{E}[xz] + \mathbb{E}[z^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[x+z]^{2} \\
&= \mathbb{E}[x^{2}] + 2\mathbb{E}[x]\mathbb{E}[z] + \mathbb{E}[z^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[x]^{2} - 2\mathbb{E}[x]\mathbb{E}[z] - \mathbb{E}[z]^{2} \\
&= \mathbb{E}[x^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[x]^{2} + \mathbb{E}[z^{2}]  - \mathbb{E}[z]^{2} \\
&= \text{var}[x] - \text{var}[z] \\
\end{split}
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.11



image.png


\[
\begin{split}
\mathbb{E}[x] &= \int\int xp(x,y) \, dx  \, dy  \\
&= \int \int xp(x|y)p(y)  \, dx  \, dy  \\
&= \int p(y) \left( \int xp(x|y) \, dx  \right)  \, dy \\
&= \int p(y)\mathbb{E}_{x}[x|y] \, dy  \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{y}[\mathbb{E}_{x}[x|y]] \\
\end{split}
\]

\[
\begin{split}
\text{var}[x] &= \mathbb{E}[x^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[x]^{2} \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{y}[\mathbb{E}_{x}[x^{2}|y]]  - \mathbb{E}[x]^{2}\\
&= \mathbb{E}_{y}[\text{var}_{x}[x|y] + (\mathbb{E}_{x}[x|y])^{2}] - \mathbb{E}[x]^{2} \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{y}[\text{var}_{x}[x|y]] + \mathbb{E}_{y}[(\mathbb{E}_{x}[x|y])^{2}] -\mathbb{E}[x]^{2} \\
&=   \mathbb{E}_{y}[\text{var}_{x}[x|y]] + \mathbb{E}_{y}[(\mathbb{E}_{x}[x|y])^{2}] -\mathbb{E}_{y}[\mathbb{E}_{x}[x|y]]^{2} \\
&=  \mathbb{E}_{y}[\text{var}_{x}[x|y]] + \text{var}_{y}[\mathbb{E}_{x}[x|y]] \\
\end{split}
\]

title: Deep Learning Foundation and Concepts Chapter02: Probabilities
text:
Exercise 2.40



Exercise 2.40


Prior Probability:

\(p(H_{\text{convex}}) = 0.1\)
\(p(H_{\text{concave}}) = 1-0.1=0.9\)

Observation:

\(N(H) = 8\)
\(N(T) = 2\)

Using Bayes’s theorem updated posterior probability
\[
P(A|B) = \frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}
\]

Calculate likelihood: \[
\begin{gather}
p(\text{data}|H_{\text{convex}}) = \binom{10}{8}(0.1)^{8}(0.9)^{2} \\
p(\text{data}|H_{\text{concave}}) = \binom{10}{8}(0.1)^{2}(0.9)^{8}
\end{gather}
\]
Marginal Likelihood: \[
p(\text{data}) = p(\text{data}|H_{\text{convex}})p(H_{\text{convex}}) + p(\text{data}|H_{\text{concave}})p(H_{\text{concave}})
\]
Calculate the posterior probability: \[
\begin{gather}
p(H_{\text{convex}}|\text{data})= \frac{p(\text{data}|H_{\text{convex}})p(H_{\text{convex}})}{p(\text{data})} \approx 0.003 \\
p(H_{\text{concave}}|\text{data})= \frac{p(\text{data}|H_{\text{concave}})p(H_{\text{concave}})}{p(\text{data})} \approx 0.997
\end{gather}
\]

So the probability of next flip landing hands is:
\[
p(H) = p(H_{\text{convex}}|\text{data})p(H_{\text{convex}}) + p(H_{\text{concave}}|\text{data})p(H_{\text{concave}}) \approx 0.900
\]

[!warning] TODO: Law of total probability

title: Post With Code V3
text: MDPs are a classical formalisation of sequential decision making, where actions influence not just immediate rewards, but also subsequent situations, or states, and through those future rewards.

title: Post With Code V3
text:
Discount Factor \(\gamma\)
It introduced to model an exponential decay in value of rewards over time. So the value function is \[
V(s) = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\textcolor{red}{\gamma^{t}}R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})
\]
According to geometric series, is the above function is guaranteed to be finite-values as long as the \(|\gamma| < 1\) \[
V(s) \leq \sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^tR_{max} = \frac {R_{max}} { 1- \gamma}
\] where, \(R_{max}\) is the maximum possible reward attainable at any given timestep in the MDP.

[!Info] The \(\gamma\) value is selected strictly from the range \(0 < \gamma < 1\) since values values in the range \(−1 < \gamma ≤ 0\) are simply not meaningful in most real-world situations

title: Post With Code V3
text:
Bellman Equation
The optimal value of a state \(s, V^{*}(s)\) is the expected value of the utility an optimally-behaving (follow policy) agent that starts in \(s\) will receive, over the rest of the agent’s lifetime. The optimal value (Q-value) of a Q-state \(Q^*(s, a)\) is the expected value of the utility an agent receives after starting in \(s\), taking \(a\) and acting optimally henceforth ^q-value






Note



Q-state, also know as action state, is the combine of agent in state \(s\), and take action \(a\)


The Bellman Equation for value function is define as follows: \[
V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s, a, s')\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V*(s')\right]
\] For Q-value function is define as follows: \[
Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} T(s, a, s')\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V*(s')\right]
\] \(R(s, a, s')\) is immediate rewards ^immediate-reward

[!Info] As showing above: \[
V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s, a)
\]

The Bellman equation is an example of a dynamic programming equation. The Q-value is a weighted sum of utilities, with each utility weighted by the probability of\(T(s, a, s')\). This define the expected utility of action optimally from Q-state \((s,a)\)
The \(V^*(s)\) is the maximum expected utility over all possible actions from \(s\).

[!question] BE usage is as a condition for optimality. In other words, if we can somehow determine a value U(s) for every state s ∈ S such that the Bellman equation holds true for each of these states, we can conclude that these values are the optimal values for their respective states. Indeed, satisfying this condition implies ∀s ∈ S, \(V(s) = V^*(s)\)


Value Iteration
To solve the optimal values, we need time-limited values. The time-limited value for a state \(s\) with a time-limit of \(k\) timesteps is denoted \(V_k(s)\), and represents the maximum expected value attainable from \(s\) given that MDP under consideration terminated in \(k\) timesteps.
Value iteration is a dynamic programming algorithm that uses an iteratively longer time limit to compute time-limited values until convergence \[\forall s,\ V_{k+1}(s) = V_k(s)\] It operates as follows: 1. \(\forall s \in S\), initialize \(V_0(s) = 0\). 2. Repeat the following update rule utile convergence: \[
\forall s \in S,\ V_{k + 1}(s) \leftarrow \max_a\sum_{s'}T(s, a, s')\left[R(s, a, s') + \gamma V_k(s') \right]
\]
The Bellman Equation gives a condition for optimality, while update rule gives a method to iteratively update values util convergence. The above function for short is \[
V_{k + 1} \leftarrow BV_k
\] where \(B\) is called the Bellman operator. which is contraction by \(\gamma\) .

Policy Extraction
The intuition behind policy extraction is very simple: if you’re in a state \(s\), you should take the action \(a\) which yields the maximum expected value.
\[
\forall s \in S, \pi^*(s) = \underset{a}{\mathrm{argmax}}Q^*(s, a)
\]

[!Info] For performance reasons that it’s better for policy extraction to have the optimal Q-values of states, in which case a single argmax operation is all that is required to determine the optimal action from a state.




Q-Value Iteration
Q-value iteration is a dynamic programming algorithm that computes time-limited Q-values. \[
Q_{k+1}(s, a) \leftarrow \sum_{s'}T(s, a, s')\left[R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_k(s', a') \right]
\tag{1}\]


Policy Iteration
Value iteration can be quite slow. At each iteration, we must update the values of all \(|S|\) states, each requires iteration over all \(|A|\) actions. The computation of each of these Q-Values, requires iteration over each of \(|S|\) states again, leading to runtime of \(O(|S|^2|A|)\)
To solve this flaw, we can use policy iteration. It operates as follows: 1. Define an initial policy. 2. Repeat the following until convergence: 1. Evaluate the current policy with policy evaluation. For a policy \(\pi\) , policy evaluation means computing \(V^{\pi}(s)\) for all states \(s\), where \(V^{\pi}\) is expected value of starting in state \(s\) when following \(\pi\): \[
V^{\pi} = \sum_{s'}T(s, \pi(s), s')\left[ R(s, \pi(s), s' )  + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\right]
\] 2. Define the policy at iteration \(i\) as \(\pi_i\). We can updated value function
\[
    V^{\pi_i}_{k+1} \leftarrow \sum_{s'}T(s, \pi(s), s')\left[ R(s, \pi(s), s' )  + \gamma V^{\pi_i}_k (s')\right]
\]
3. Once evaluate the current policy, use **policy improvement** to generate a better policy: 
\[
\pi_{i+1}(s) = \underset{a}{\mathrm{argmax}} \sum_{s'}T(s, a, s')\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi_i}(s') \right]
\]

title: Stanford EE364A Convex Optimization Lecture 01: Introduction
text:
\[
\begin{gather}
\text{minimize} & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to} &  f_i(x) \leq 0 \quad i =  1, \dots, m \\
& g_i(x) = 0 \quad i =  1  \dots, p \\
\end{gather}
\]

\(x \in \mathbf{R}^n\) is (vector) variable to be chosen to
$f_0: ^n $ is the objective function to be minimized or (maxizimed)
\(f_1, \dots, f_m: \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) are the inequality constraint functions
\(g_1, \dots, g_p\) are equality constraint functions

Solution or optimal point \(x^{\star}\) has smallest value of \(f_0\) among all vector that staisfy the constraints
For example, in machine learning problem: * \(x\) represents the parametrs in a model * the constraints impose requirements on model parameters (e.g. nonnegativity) * The objective function \(f_0(x)\) is the sum of two terms: * a prediction error(also called loss) on some observed data * a (regularization) term that penelized model complexity

title: Stanford EE364A Convex Optimization Lecture 01: Introduction
text:
Lear-Squared Problems
\[
\text{minimize} \quad || Ax - b ||_{2}^{2}
\]
Solving least squares problems: * Analytical solution (closed form): \(x^{\star} = (A^\mathrm{T}A)^{-1}A^\mathrm{T}b\) The computation time proportional to \(n^2k (A\in \mathbf{R}^{k \times n})\)

Least-Squares problems are easy to recognization (e.g. the regression problem)
A few standard techniques increase flexible :

Including weights
Adding regularization terms

title: Stanford EE364A Convex Optimization Lecture 01: Introduction
text:
Linear Programming
$$ \[\begin{gather}
\text{minimize} & c^{\mathrm{T}}x \\
\text{subject to} &  a_{i}^{\mathrm{T}}x \leq b_i \quad i =  1, \dots, m \\

\end{gather}\] $$
Solving linear programs: * No analytical formula for solution * Computation time porportional to \(n^2m\) if \(m \geq n\)
Using Linear programming: * Not as easy as recognize as least-square problems * A few standard tricks used to convert problem into linear programming * Problems involving \(\ell_1-\) or \(\ell_{\infty}-\) norms * Piecewise-lienar function

title: Stanford EE364A Convex Optimization Lecture 01: Introduction
text:
Convex Optimization problem
\[
\begin{gather}
\text{minimize} & f_{0}(x) \\
\text{subject to} &  f_{i}(x) \leq b_i \quad i =  1, \dots, m \\
\end{gather}
\]

Objective \(f_0\) and constraint functions \(f_i(x)\) are convex:

\[
\begin{align}
& f_i(\alpha x + \beta y)  \leq \alpha f_i(x) + \beta f_i(y)   \\
& \alpha + \beta  = 1 \text{ and } \alpha  \geq 0 , \beta  \geq 0
\end{align}
\] Include Least-Square problems and linear programs as special case.
Solving convex optimzation problems * No analytical solution
Using convex optimizatoin * Often difficult to recognize * many tricks for transforming problems into convex form * Almost any problem can be solved via convex optimization
